2005-10-01.title: Life for Feeding Man to Lions
2005-10-01.abstract: To the whistles and ululations of a packed courtroom, a white farmer who ordered a black former worker beaten and thrown to lions 20 months ago was sentenced to life imprisonment, and his accomplice was sentenced to 15 years. The farmer, Mark Scott-Crossley, above, became a symbol of what some say is enduring racism in South Africa after his crime was revealed in early 2004. His lawyers had said his crime was rooted in a life filled with psychological trauma, but the judge said a crime so monstrous demanded the harshest sentence. The dead farm worker, Nelson Chisale, was beaten with sticks and tied for seven hours to a tree before being loaded into a pickup and driven to a reserve of rare white lions, where he was tossed over the fence. The police were able to retrieve only scraps of clothing and bone fragments from the pen.    Michael Wines (NYT)

2005-10-01.title: Catalonia Seeks Status as 'Nation'
2005-10-01.abstract: Catalonia's parliament approved a plan that declares the northeastern region of seven million people a largely autonomous ''nation'' within Spain, setting the stage for a possible confrontation with the central government. The proposal calls for transferring an array of powers, like tax collection, away from the central government. It now goes to the national Parliament in Madrid, where passage is not being ruled out because of the Catalan government's strong ties with several parties there.    Renwick McLean (NYT)

2005-10-01.title: Simultaneous bombs rock tourist areas in Bali
Names of the Dead
2005-10-01.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,928 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans this week:    ARNOLD, Daniel L., 27, Staff Sgt., Army National Guard; Montrose, Pa.; 28th Infantry Division.    BENFORD, Jason A., 30, Staff Sgt., Army; Toledo, Ohio; Third Infantry Division.    BROWN, Oliver J., 19, Pfc., Army National Guard; Carbondale, Pa.; 28th Infantry Division.    JACOBSON, Elizabeth N., 21, Airman First Class, Air Force; Riviera Beach, Fla.; 17th Security Forces Squadron.    MORIN, Steve Jr., 34, Sgt., Army National Guard; Arlington, Tex.; 36th Infantry Division.    PUGLIESE, George A., 39, Staff Sgt., Army National Guard; Carbondale, Pa.; 28th Infantry Division.    SLEBODNIK, Eric W., 21, Sgt., Army National Guard; Greenfield Township, Pa.; 28th Infantry Division.    WIEGAND, Lee A., 20, Specialist, Army National Guard; Hallstead, Pa.; 28th Infantry Division.

2005-10-01.title: In Morocco, a Rights Movement, at the King's Pace
2005-10-01.abstract: More than most people, Ahmed Marzouki lived the horror of what often passes for the law in the Arab world.      He was locked away for more than 18 years of solitary confinement inside Tazmamart, a desert fortress built specifically for 58 military officers who had minor roles in two attempted coups. A military court had initially sentenced him to five years.

2005-10-02.title: Bush team is criticized for 'covert propaganda'
Fatah Outpolls Hamasin Palestinian Elections
2005-10-02.abstract: In final results of West Bank elections in 104 municipalities, the dominant Fatah faction won control of 51 councils, while the radical Islamic group Hamas won 13, officials said Saturday.     Other factions, mostly local groupings of large families or clans, won control of the other 40 councils, according to Jamal al-Shobaki, head of the Palestinian election commission, who released the final figures to reporters in Ramallah.

2005-10-02.title: Turkey's Quest to Join Europe Collides With Its Textile Dreams
2005-10-02.abstract: As the muezzin calls worshipers to morning prayers in this former capital of the Ottoman Empire, a small army of women in head scarves is sewing the final stitches on Adidas sweat pants and Guess T-shirts at the sprawling Biesseci factory here.     They are the foot soldiers of Turkey's quest to get its textiles onto the runways of Milan, the shelves of London boutiques and the racks of American shopping malls.

2005-10-03.title: Venezuelan Strongman's New Gig: National Disc Jockey
2005-10-03.abstract: Deep in Venezuela's new, cumbersome Social Responsibility Law is an item that requires radio stations to play more -- much more -- Venezuelan music. The idea, the fiercely nationalist government says, is to promote Venezuelan culture over foreign culture, particularly American rock, which has dominated radio airplay for years.     If the measure seems obscure, its effects have not been. From the techno-pop wizards of cosmopolitan Caracas to the folksy crooners of this cattle town, Venezuelan musicians say they are reaping benefits from President Hugo Chávez's efforts to regulate culture.

2005-10-03.title: UN gives Croatia the green light
European Phone Companies Begin Merger Talks
2005-10-03.abstract: Telefónica of Spain is in preliminary talks to acquire KPN of the Netherlands for about $24 billion, in a transaction that could spark another round of consolidation in Europe's telecommunications industry, according to executives involved in the discussions.      The talks are at a ''very preliminary stage,'' one the executives cautioned. But even if no deal is reached, the discussions themselves could force rival companies to begin their own merger negotiations. The European telecommunications industry remains widely fragmented despite a spate of deals five years ago among wireless companies.

2005-10-04.title: World Briefing | Europe: Monitors For Ukraine-Moldova Border
2005-10-04.abstract: European Union will dispatch monitors along border of Ukraine and Moldova, including frontier with separatist Trans-Dniester region, in effort to reduce smuggling and arms trafficking; 50 European customs and border officers will begin operations and training Dec 1 (S)

2005-10-04.title: U.S. takes offensive in rebel haven
A Mideast both observant and watchful
At news conference, Bush backs Miers

2005-10-05.title: World Briefing | Asia: Taiwan: Protest Over Google Map
2005-10-05.abstract: Foreign Ministry of Taiwan asks Internet search engine Google to change name of its country as it appears on map, 'Taiwan, Province of China,' but does not offer alternative (S)

2005-10-05.title: 2 EU leaders raise red flags on Turkey
Naval project of Italy and France falters
U.S. Envoy Warns Against Deposing Nicaragua's Leader
2005-10-05.abstract: Robert B. Zoellick, the American deputy secretary of state, intended to help defuse a political crisis.

2005-10-05.title: World Briefing | Americas: Venezuela: Government To Make 'Bolivarian Computers'
2005-10-05.abstract: Venezuelan government is working with China to produce about 80,000 'Bolivarian computers' in 2006, priced to be affordable to as many Venezuelans as possible; computers will be made by new state company Technological Industries of Venezuela (S)

2005-10-06.title: Blast Kills 10 in Iraq
2005-10-06.abstract: Iraqi National Assembly, bowing to strong pressure from United Nations, votes to cancel last-minute rule change that all but guaranteed passage of new constitution in Oct 15 referendum; Sunni Arab leaders had accused Shiite and Kurdish leaders of trying to rig referendum; Shiite leaders who vote to cancel rule change say they reserve right to challenge results if constitution fails in vote; their fear is that insurgents might manipulate vote by attacking Shiites to keep them away from polls; dispute sharpens sectarian divisions over constitution, and uneasy resolution seems to open door to further dissent, especially if violence worsens; in Washington, State Dept spokesman calls reversal of controversial rule 'a positive step'; photos (M)

2005-10-06.title: UN failed to coordinate tsunami relief, Red Cross report says
Briefly: Vaccine proves effective against cervical cancer
2 tongues, once wed, drifting slowly apart
Germans to Wait Out Weekend for New Chancellor
2005-10-06.abstract: But by the end of the weekend or on Monday morning, officials said, the question of who will be chancellor will be "definitely answered."

2005-10-07.title: Political tension mounts in Germany ahead of a deal
Al Qaeda Tells Ally in Iraq To Strive for Global Goals
2005-10-07.abstract: The second-ranking leader of Al Qaeda has warned the top militant in Iraq that attacks on civilians and videotaped executions committed by his followers threaten to jeopardize the broader extremist cause, a senior United States official said Thursday.     The warning, from Ayman al-Zawahiri to Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, was spelled out in a 6,000-word letter, dated early in July, that was obtained by American forces conducting counterterrorism operations in Iraq, the official said in a briefing.

2005-10-07.title: Abbas Says He Will Press Sharon on Withdrawal From West Bank
2005-10-07.abstract: The Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas, said Thursday that when he meets with Prime Minister Ariel Sharon, his most important demand would be that Israel carry out the measures the two men agreed on in February.     But Mr. Abbas cast doubt on when they would meet, telling reporters in Gaza City that ''the date has not yet been decided,'' despite remarks by King Abdullah II of Jordan, Israeli and even Palestinian officials that the meeting would take place next Tuesday, probably in Jerusalem.

2005-10-07.title: Briefly: Katrina repair contracts reopened for bidding
Europa: It's not just Turkey that must look at changing

2005-10-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Editor Guilty Of Insulting The State
2005-10-08.abstract: An Istanbul court convicted a Turkish-Armenian journalist of insulting Turkish identity in a series of articles he wrote last year. The journalist, Hrant Dink, left, the editor of the bilingual Turkish and Armenian weekly Agos, was given a six-month suspended prison sentence. In the articles, he urged Armenians to rid themselves of the poisoning effect of their historical anger toward Turks. Prosecutors argued that he had implied that Turkish blood was poison.   Sebnem Arsu (NYT)

2005-10-08.title: Italians fret about an end to La Dolce Vita
World Briefing | Americas: Peru: Arrest Would Welcome Ex-Leader
2005-10-08.abstract: Officials reacted to former President Alberto K. Fujimori's pledge from his self-exile in Japan that he would run in the presidential election in April with reminders that he still risked arrest if he returned. Vice President David Waisman promised that he would greet him at the airport with a pair of handcuffs. Mr. Fujimori is wanted in Peru on charges of corruption and of sanctioning paramilitary death squads. He fled to Japan, his ancestral homeland, in 2000 after 10 years in power. Japan has long refused to extradite him because he has Japanese citizenship.   Juan Forero (NYT)

2005-10-08.title: Muslim Rivalry Leaves 8 Dead in an Attack in Pakistan
2005-10-08.abstract: At least 8 people were killed and 20 wounded Friday in an attack on a congregation of a minority Muslim sect, the Qadiani, also known as Ahmadis, who are considered heretics by orthodox Muslims.      The attack, in central Pakistan, raised fresh concerns about the protection of minorities in Pakistan, a majority Sunni Muslim state.

2005-10-08.title: A Wine Baroness Rebuilds a Brand
2005-10-08.abstract: TWICE in its long history, Château Margaux has been rescued by women.      The first, Laure de Fumel, acquired it in 1795, during the turbulence following the French Revolution, and managed to save it and its vineyards. But after a series of forgettable vintages she despaired of making great wine and auctioned it off in 1802.

2005-10-09.title: New Cases of Avian Flu Are Reported in Europe
2005-10-09.abstract: Asian bird flu appeared to continue its westward spread with reports of two outbreaks in birds in Romania and Turkey.

2005-10-09.title: Morocco Finds Graves of 50 Killed in '70's
2005-10-09.abstract: An official Moroccan human rights group announced Saturday that it had discovered the graves of 50 political prisoners who died at secret detention camps in the 1970's.     The group, the Equity and Reconciliation Commission, was set up by royal decree by King Mohammed VI to help discover what happened to hundreds of Moroccans arrested and tortured by security agents in the 1970's, while the country was fighting separatists in Western Sahara. No official figures exist on how many people disappeared, but rights groups say it was up to 600.

2005-10-09.title: Desperate parents struggle to reach buriedchildren
Belgium Wrestles With Counterterrorism Efforts
2005-10-09.abstract: A wide distribution of terrorist threats has presented small countries like Belgium with difficult law enforcement problems.

2005-10-10.title: As Polar Ice Turns to Water, Dreams of Treasure Abound
2005-10-10.abstract: With the thawing of the polar ice caps, the Arctic is undergoing a great rush for virgin territory and natural resources.

2005-10-10.title: As Polar Ice Turns to Water, Dreams of Treasure Abound
2005-10-10.abstract: With the thawing of the polar ice caps, the Arctic is undergoing a great rush for virgin territory and natural resources.

2005-10-10.title: Angela Merkel Followed a Political Path Less Traveled
2005-10-10.abstract: Angela Merkel's journey has left political analysts grasping for what she might do as chancellor.

2005-10-10.title: Looting breaks out in Pakistan-held Kashmir
NATO envoys to discuss using peacekeepers in Afghanistan

2005-10-11.title: Angela Merkel: Politician Who Can Show a Flash of Steel
2005-10-11.abstract: Mrs. Merkel is poised to become the first female chancellor of Germany and the first eastern German to lead the reunified nation.

2005-10-11.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Squatters Win Court Reprieve
2005-10-11.abstract: Zimbabwe's High Court issues preliminary order barring government from removing squatters from Mbare, suburb of capital city Harare; court says squatters should not be moved until promises of Pres Robert G Mugabe to relocate them are fulfilled (S)

2005-10-11.title: Merkel will lead as Schröder yields chancellorship
World Briefing | Europe: Ukraine: Criminal Case Against Ex-Security Chief
2005-10-11.abstract: Ukranian national security adviser Petro O Poroshenko, one of Pres Viktor A Yushchenko's closest aides, faces criminal corruption charges (S)

2005-10-11.title: Britain May Ban 15 Militant Groups
2005-10-11.abstract: The British government unveiled plans on Monday to outlaw 15 foreign Islamic militant groups as part of its campaign against terrorism following the July bombings here.     Home Secretary Charles Clarke, Britain's senior law enforcement official, published a list of the groups and said he would seek Parliament's approval to ''proscribe'' them under counterterrorism laws approved in 2000.

2005-10-12.title: A Big Debate on Little People: Ancient Species or Modern Dwarfs?
2005-10-12.abstract: Discoveries in Indonesia give additional support to the idea that a separate species of little people now extinct lived there.

2005-10-12.title: Pentagon Orders Additional Aid For Guatemala
2005-10-12.abstract: The Pentagon is sending additional helicopters and possibly a medical team to storm-ravaged Guatemala to help relief operations there, defense officials said Tuesday.      The military will send six more CH-47 Chinook and UH-60 Black Hawk helicopters from Soto Cano Air Base in Honduras, bringing to 15 the number of American helicopters assisting the Guatemala mission, said Roger Pardo-Maurer, the Pentagon's top Latin American policy official.

2005-10-12.title: Liberians Line Up to Vote, Saying Long Wait Is Worth It
2005-10-12.abstract: They came carrying benches to sit on for the long hours of waiting, umbrellas to shield them from the harsh sun and punishing rain, and above all the small laminated cards that give each man and woman in this long-suffering nation the chance to elect new leaders on Tuesday to guide them beyond a decade and a half of war.      ''This election is the only thing that can save us from more war,'' said Jonathan Lephas, a 41-year-old farmer who waited to cast his ballot in a camp for displaced people in Kakata, about an hour northeast of Monrovia, the capital. ''I will wait here the whole night to vote. We need peace.''

2005-10-12.title: Hundreds gather to remember Bali bombings
African Food for Africa's Starving Is Roadblocked in Congress
2005-10-12.abstract: It seemed like a no-brainer: changing the law to allow the federal government to buy food in Africa for Africans facing starvation instead of paying enormous sums to ship it from the American heartland, halfway around the world. Not only would the food get to the hungry in weeks instead of months, the government would save money and help African farmers at the same time.      The new approach had an impeccable sponsor in Republican-dominated Washington. The Bush administration, famous for its go-it-alone style, was trying to move the United States -- by far the world's biggest food donor -- into the international mainstream with a proposal to take a step in just this direction. A lot of rich countries had already done so, most recently Canada.

2005-10-13.title: Fatal strain of bird flu confirmed in Turkey
Time Is Running Out for Quake Survivors, Relief Official Says
2005-10-13.abstract: Many parts of Pakistan stayed beyond the reach of rescuers, leaving survivors in desperate need of food and shelter.

2005-10-13.title: Somalia Pirates Seize Another Aid Ship
2005-10-13.abstract: Second cargo ship carrying relief supplies to Somalia for World Food Program has been hijacked (S)

2005-10-13.title: In a videoconference, Bush rallies the troops

2005-10-14.title: Muslim Charities Re-emerge for Quake Victims
2005-10-14.abstract: All across America, Muslim charities, many of which have shunned the spotlight since 9/11 lest they attract unwanted law enforcement attention, are now stepping up their efforts to raise money for the victims of the earthquake that crumbled the northernmost corner of Pakistan.     In many cases, they have been more successful than their mainstream charitable counterparts, many of which have said that donors are not responding to their appeals for contributions for Pakistan.

2005-10-14.title: Indian Company to Make Generic Version of Flu Drug Tamiflu
2005-10-14.abstract: Cipla of Bombay, major Indian drug company, says it would start making generic version of Tamiflu, anti-infuenza drug in critically short supply, in face of possible epidemic of avian flu; Dr Yusuf K Hamied, Cipla's chairman, acknowledges he might face fight in Indian courts with Roche, Swiss pharmaceutical giant that holds patent; patent laws across world allow governments to cancel patents during emergencies and either buy generics or force patent holders to license their formulas to rivals; Roche has been under growing pressure from several countries and United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan to license generic versions of Tamiflu (M)

2005-10-14.title: Britain's Secret Service Indeed! Spy on It on Its Web site
2005-10-14.abstract: Britain's Secret Intelligence Service, or MI6, introduces its first publicly accessible Web site, opening it to modicum of scrutiny; service, made famous in James Bond movies, was once so secret that it denied its own existence; interest is so intense that new Web site records 3.5 million hits in first few hours; photos (M)

2005-10-14.title: Needs of Quake Survivors Overwhelm India and Pakistan
2005-10-14.abstract: With temperatures plummeting, India faced an acute shortage of tents, leaving earthquake survivors in villages of Kashmir to spend their seventh night outdoors.

2005-10-15.title: Iraq battles are seen spilling into Syria
Poll Workers in Rebel City: Danger 'Has a Purpose' Now
2005-10-15.abstract: Clutching net bags of overnight essentials with boxes of ballots under their arms, about 700 poll workers climbed aboard giant armored trucks on Friday and rode along one of Iraq's most dangerous highways to voting sites all over this city.      The workers -- mostly poor Shiites from southern Iraq and slums in Baghdad -- came to run polling stations here in Ramadi, the capital of Anbar Province and the center of Iraq's violent insurgency. The pay was low ($300 for 10 days) considering that the risk was high (as of 7 p.m., the marines had found at least six roadside bombs in the western part of the city alone).

2005-10-15.title: A Small, Obscure and This Year, Very Busy Air Force Unit
2005-10-15.abstract: A unit that specializes in swiftly loading cargo on and off massive transport planes was at the scene of two of this year's most catastrophic natural disasters.

2005-10-15.title: World Briefing | Australia: Off-Shore Refugee Camp Empties
2005-10-15.abstract: Australia announced that 25 Afghan and Iraq refugees who have been detained by the government on the bleak island of Nauru for more than three years would be allowed to come to Australia, 12 permanently and 13 while their claims for asylum are reviewed. At one time, Australia held more than 200 men, women and children behind wire on the tiny island, as part of its effort to crack down on ''boat people'' trying to reach Australia. Senator Bob Brown of the Greens Party called on the Nauru camp to be closed permanently, but Prime Minister John Howard, calling the use of Nauru an ''outstanding success,'' said it would remain open for possible use in the future. Two refugees remain in detention there.   Raymond Bonner (NYT)

2005-10-16.title: Briefs: Emergency is lifted as weather woes fade
A Diplomatic Weekend of Foreign Policy and Alabama Football
2005-10-16.abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice announced Saturday that Jack Straw, the British foreign secretary, had accepted her invitation to spend Friday, Saturday and Sunday in her home state, Alabama, for ''an important diplomatic weekend,'' as an aide described it.      They will perform the pregame coin flip when the University of Alabama football team faces Tennessee in Tuscaloosa, and then stay to watch the game, said Jim Wilkinson, her senior adviser. Their other activities will include a visit to Brunetta C. Hill Elementary School in Birmingham, which Ms. Rice attended.

2005-10-16.title: Romania begins culling of birds in Danube delta

2005-10-17.title: Azerbaijan foils return of an opposition leader
Saddam Gets His Day in Court
2005-10-17.abstract: The trial of former Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein begins on Wednesday. The tribunal, part victors' justice, part reprocessing of history, could end sooner than expected, with the defendant possibly facing the gallows after only the first of 12 planned trials.

2005-10-17.title: West Bank travel curbs follow shootings
Obituary: Ba Jin, Chinese novelist and anarchist

2005-10-18.title: U.S. antiterrorism envoy challenged in Indonesia
World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Deaths In Rebel Attack At 135: Chechen Claims Responsibility
2005-10-18.abstract: Attack by armed Chechen separatists on police and security buildings in southern Russian city of Nalchik kills 35 officers and 9 civilians; Russian officials claim 91 attackers were killed and 36 captured; separatist leader Shamil Basayev claims responsibility for raid and says only 41 of his forces were killed (S)

2005-10-18.title: World Briefing | Australia: Plan To Double Domestic Spy Staff
2005-10-18.abstract: Australian Prime Minister John Howard announces doubling number of spies in Australian Security Intelligence Organization, to 1,860, over next five years; move is in response to recent proposals to toughen antiterrorism laws and many of new agents will be recruited from Muslim population (S)

2005-10-18.title: Azerbaijan Leader Predicts Victory and Scoffs at 'Revolution'
2005-10-18.abstract: President Ilham Aliyev predicted today that his party would win overwhelmingly in parliamentary elections next month.

2005-10-19.title: Mubarak Foe, Bravado Gone, Feels Smeared
2005-10-19.abstract: Ayman Nour, the runner-up in Egypt's presidential voting last month, says he is now the target of a campaign to discredit him.

2005-10-19.title: Rumsfeld Warns Young Chinese on Isolationism
2005-10-19.abstract: Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld urges next generation of China's Communist leadership to become 'major player' in global economy by taking greater responsibility for international system and not just reaping financial rewards, speech to mid-career officials in Beijing; warns against building new Great Wall that restricts free expression and says military expansion raises questions about China's choices with regard to regional stability; counsels against isolationism; visit is Rumsfeld's first as defense secretary; photo (M)

2005-10-19.title: Merkel and aide share political past
Pride and Politics: India Rejects Quake Aid
2005-10-19.abstract: India insists that it needs no international aid in aftermath of earthquake, even though fewer than half of survivors are estimated to have tents as temperatures fall in mountains of Kashmir; officials, who have even sent aid to archrival Pakistan in their determination to portray India as world power, face anger in Kashmir among people forced to build own tents out of wreckage; photo (M)

2005-10-19.title: U.S. Is Pressing Palestinians To Bar Extremist Candidates
2005-10-19.abstract: Bush administration, worried that Palestinian militants will gain political foothold in January elections, presses Mahmoud Abbas to require candidates to renounce violence and other unlawful methods; Abbas meets in Paris with Lebanese Prime Min Fouad Siniora about Palestinian militias that use Lebanon as base for attacking Israel (M)

2005-10-20.title: Old Ways of Life Are Fading as the Arctic Thaws
2005-10-20.abstract: For the four million people who live in the Arctic, a changing climate is threatening their environment, homes and traditions.

2005-10-20.title: Agreement reached to open Kashmir border
3 U.S. soldiers face arrest in Spain
Parties agree change needed in Germany
Old Ways of Life Are Fading as the Arctic Thaws
2005-10-20.abstract: Article in series The Big Melt describes profound effect warmer climate will have on four million people living in Arctic region; most of their homes are on frozen ground known as permafrost; as it melts and thins, homes and infrastructure are at risk of serious damage; across Arctic, indigenous tribes with traditions shaped by centuries of living in extremes of cold and ice are noticing changes in weather and wildlife; are trying to adapt, but it can be confounding; Inuit have used Arctic Ocean ice as highway, building material and hunting platform; retreat of sea ice is making it hard to preserve their connections to tradition; most Arctic climate experts say warming trend is driven by heat-trapping emissions and is unlikely to reverse; many scientists and officials in Russia predict warming of last 30 years will give way to new period of cold; Norway and seven other Arctic nations are discussing ways to protect environment they share in forum called Arctic Council, but guidelines it issues come with no enforcement powers; photos; map; chart (L)

2005-10-21.title: North Korea Ready for Talks, U.S. Envoy Says
2005-10-21.abstract: Gov Bill Richardson says North Korea is 'fully committed' to returning to nuclear disarmament talks and showing 'flexibility' on conditions for obtaining light-water reactor, interview in Tokyo; photo; says tone on his fourth visit to Pyongyang was most positive yet; says North Koreans also agree to allow most foreign aid workers to remain, including those providing food to about third of population; describes destitute countryside; says there were no spent fuel rods apparent at nuclear research complex that is usually off-limits; has often visited such sites in New Mexico (M)

2005-10-21.title: Briefly: Talks open with U.S. on nuclear cooperation
U.S. Official Urges Fair Elections in Azerbaijan
2005-10-21.abstract: US Assistant Sec of State Daniel Fried urges Azerbaijan to ensure fair parliamentary elections in November, expressing US concern over reports of pre-election abuses and poor preparation for elections (S)

2005-10-21.title: Ex-Powell aide assails Bush's foreign policy
Rhein-Main Air Base Journal; After 60 Years, the Yanks Fly Out, Leaving Just the Ghosts
2005-10-21.abstract: Rhein-Main Air Base in Germany is closing down after decades as American military's gateway to Europe; photo; map; US consulate has left central Frankfurt for fortified complex on outskirts, further lowering US profile in city that since World War II has been most American in Germany; base closing follows changing economic and geopolitical realities, including relationship chilled by German opposition to Iraq war; Frankfurt Airport, which shares site, has pushed for years to take over and build more passenger facilities (M)

2005-10-22.title: Debate but Little Finality on Mideast Streets
2005-10-22.abstract: After months of anticipation, the first findings of the United Nations investigation of the killing of Rafik Hariri, the former prime minister of Lebanon, overtook Beirut and Damascus on Friday, dominating conversations and blaring in news broadcasts at almost every restaurant and shop.      Many Lebanese stayed home or in their offices, fearing violence resulting from the release of the report. Perhaps the only thing selling well in many popular commercial districts was a special edition of the newspaper Al Mustaqbal, owned by the Hariri family, which published the 54-page report in its entirety in Arabic. Booksellers and newspaper stands sold out of the special issue within minutes, and some people even took to scalping it for five times the price.

2005-10-22.title: Rice, in Alabama, Draws Parallels for Democracy Everywhere
2005-10-22.abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice, in an unusual evocation on Friday of her own past to make a point about the contemporary world, visited the segregated school that she attended and the university where Gov. George Wallace barred the door to blacks, declaring that Alabama had progressed by ''light years'' since those days.     It was a dramatic setting for her message, repeated for months in Muslim countries and elsewhere, often in the face of considerable skepticism: it is possible for Iraq and other societies to throw off their legacies of violence and oppression to become viable democracies.

2005-10-22.title: Opponents Move to Impeach Malawi President
2005-10-22.abstract: President Bingu wa Mutharika, accused by critics of poor performance and of using public money to cement his power, has been summoned to appear before Parliament on Thursday to answer impeachment charges, its speaker said. Members of the president's party walked out of Parliament in protest, saying that the summons violated parliamentary rules and that he would not comply. The president has been in political hot water almost since he took office in May 2004, when he split from his backer and predecessor as president, Bakili Muluzi, and founded his own party. Mr. Mutharika is struggling to deal with Malawi's drought-induced food crisis, which has left as many as four million of its citizens in danger of severe hunger.   Michael Wines (NYT)

2005-10-22.title: Pakistanis Unite to Aid Victims of Quake
2005-10-22.abstract: All around this leveled town, thousands of pieces of clothing sit in great heaps, creating bright pockets of color amid fields of gray rubble. A checkered maroon sweater. A pair of bluejeans. A pink shirt. At first glance, the clothing appears to be a sad vestige of the thousands who perished in this mountain town. Instead, it is a testament to the living.     In what some Pakistanis are calling the greatest display of national unity in their country's 58-year-history, thousands of volunteers from across the country spontaneously collected vast amounts of food, clothing and medicine and rushed it to northern Pakistan after the severe earthquake of Oct. 8. The piles are the product of an impromptu grass-roots relief effort that Pakistanis say they have never seen before.

2005-10-22.title: Lawyer's Slaying Raises Questions on Hussein Trial
2005-10-22.abstract: The execution-style killing of a defense lawyer in the trial of Saddam Hussein and some top associates shocked Iraqi and American officials on Friday and renewed doubts about whether it is possible to hold a fair trial in the midst of a war that has spurred a wave of revenge killings against people linked to Mr. Hussein.     A group of a dozen armed men seized the lawyer, Sadoun al-Janabi, from his Baghdad office at 10 p.m. on Thursday and his body, with two bullet wounds to the head, was found in a rubbish-strewn lot nearby about an hour later, an Iraqi police spokesman said. The killing occurred less than 36 hours after Mr. Hussein's trial began on Wednesday, with live television coverage that identified Mr. Janabi by name and showed close-ups of him presenting arguments in the court on behalf of his client, Awad Hamed al-Bander, the former head of the Revolutionary Court under Mr. Hussein.

2005-10-23.title: In Chilling U.N. Assassination Report, Fake Assassin and Intricate Plot by Top Syrians
2005-10-23.abstract: Often reading like a paperback thriller, the United Nations report traces months of plotting the assassination of Lebanon's former prime minister.

2005-10-23.title: Egyptian Police Guard Coptic Church Attacked by Muslims
2005-10-23.abstract: Riot police forces armed with shotguns guarded a Coptic Christian church here on Saturday, a day after Muslim protesters tried to storm the building in a demonstration that was broken up when security forces fired tear gas and rubber bullets into the crowd.     Three people were killed and many more injured in what officials called the worst case of sectarian violence to strike this Mediterranean city in recent memory. The streets remained tense Saturday, and many people warned that foreigners were not welcome. The mood in the city was sour and explosive.

2005-10-23.title: Unseen Enemy Is at Its Fiercest in a Sunni City
2005-10-23.abstract: The Bradley fighting vehicles moved slowly down this city's main boulevard. Suddenly, a homemade bomb exploded, punching into one vehicle. Then another explosion hit, briefly lifting a second vehicle up onto its side before it dropped back down again.      Two American soldiers climbed out of a hatch, the first with his pant leg on fire, and the other completely in flames. The first rolled over to help the other man, but when they touched, the first man also burst into flames. Insurgent gunfire began to pop.

2005-10-24.title: Brazilian Voters Appear to Have Beaten Ban on Weapons
2005-10-24.abstract: In a national referendum on gun control, voters in Brazil appear to have overwhelmingly rejected a sweeping permanent prohibition on the sale of arms and ammunition.     With three-quarters of the vote counted there, the gun ban proposal, endorsed by President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva and scores of actors, pop stars and other celebrities, was trailing by a ratio of almost two to one. Voting was obligatory, and nearly 120 million people in a country long plagued by high rates of crime and violence were expected to cast ballots.

2005-10-24.title: In the Twilight, the Gentle Strumming of a Bygone Iraq
2005-10-24.abstract: Outside in the darkness of western Baghdad, gunfire and ominous thumping noises could be heard. But inside his exquisitely appointed living room, Husham al-Madfai was pouring a glass of Scotch and welcoming his guests. They had come for dinner and a private performance by Salman Shukur, Iraq's last living traditional master of the oud, or Arab lute.     ''Salman!'' Mr. Madfai called out, as the guest of honor, a small, white-haired man with a crinkled smile, stepped slowly across the marble floor. ''Welcome, welcome.''

2005-10-24.title: Looters hit Cancun as Mexico tries to clean up

2005-10-25.title: UN food agency to stop aid work in North Korea
Study Finds Small Developing Lands Hit Hardest by 'Brain Drain'
2005-10-25.abstract: World Bank study finds poor countries across Africa, Central America and Caribbean are losing staggering portions of their college-educated workers to wealthy democracies; analysts say study documents troubling pattern of 'brain drain,' flight of skilled middle-class workers who could help lift their countries out of poverty; chart (M)

2005-10-25.title: U.N. Envoy Sharply Criticizes South Africa's AIDS Program
2005-10-25.abstract: Race Against Time, new book by Stephen Lewis, United Nations' special envoy to Africa on AIDS, singles out South Africa's government and Pres Thabo Mbeki for what he calls bewildering policies and lackadaisical approach to treatment of nation's millions of HIV-positive citizens; holds that virtually every other nation in eastern and southern Africa is 'working harder at treatment,' and with relatively fewer resources than South Africa; Lewis claims he has been banned from traveling to South Africa unless he apologizes for his remarks (M)

2005-10-25.title: Zimbabwe's Opposition Party Splits Over Running in Next Election
2005-10-25.abstract: Members of political party that poses only serious opposition to Pres Robert G Mugabe of Zimbabwe defy their leader, Morgan Tsvangirai, and file as candidates for election to country's new senate; Tsvangirai said party should not take part because that would only add stamp of legitimacy to results of vote that is certain to be rigged in favor of Mugabe's party; opposition party members claim boycotting election would cede political control to Mugabe; party has been roiled for last week by public broadsides between two factions (M)

2005-10-25.title: Rosa Parks, civil rights pioneer, dies

2005-10-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Greece: European Court Rules Feta Is Greek
2005-10-26.abstract: European Union's highest court awards Greece sole rights to production of feta cheese, protecting name of traditional Greek product (S)

2005-10-26.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Gaza Strip: Israel Fires Missiles After Rocket Attacks
2005-10-26.abstract: Israel launches missile strike at building in Gaza belonging to Al Aksa Martyrs Brigades and at another linked to Islamic Jihad (S)

2005-10-26.title: Relief workers plead for more aid
U.S. agrees to Okinawa base relocation
For Devout Pakistani Muslims, Aid Muddles Loyalties
2005-10-26.abstract: Anecdotal interviews with earthquake survivors in Pakistani mountain district of Mansehra suggest that American assistance may be improving Pakistanis' perceptions of United States--image that has been overwhelmingly negative since invasions of Afghanistan and Iraq; international aid may sway perceptions of moderate Pakistanis, particularly well-educated city dwellers, but it is unlikley to sway country's small core of militants who support Al Qaeda; map; photo (L)

2005-10-27.title: Briefs: Ex-aide to Chirac guilty of corruption charges
World Briefing | Europe: Switzerland: Russian Convicted Of Killing Air Controller
2005-10-27.abstract: Zurich court convicts Russian Vitaly Kaloyev of murdering Swiss air-traffic controller Pieter Nelson and sentences him to eight years in prison; Kaloyev blamed Nelson for deaths of his wife and children in 2002 crash (S)

2005-10-27.title: World Briefing | Europe: Vatican City: Pope Sends His Foreign Minister To Moscow
2005-10-27.abstract: Vatican's foreign minister Archbishop Giovanni Lajolo begins visit to Russia to strengthen diplomatic relations and seek closer ties to Orthodox Church (S)

2005-10-27.title: Entr'acte: An old weapon revived: U.S. cultural diplomacy
Germans Convict 4 in Plot to Hit at Jewish Sites
2005-10-27.abstract: A German court on Wednesday convicted three Middle Eastern men of belonging to a terrorist group linked to Al Qaeda and of plotting to attack Jewish targets in Germany. A fourth man was found guilty of supporting the group.      The defendants -- two Jordanians, a Palestinian and an Algerian -- were sentenced to between five and eight years in prison by the court in Düsseldorf, Germany, which said the men were taking orders from Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, a terrorist leader at the center of the bloody insurgency in Iraq.

2005-10-28.title: Russia Calls Evidence in Oil-for-Food Report Forged
2005-10-28.abstract: Oil industry analysts here have shrugged off the findings, noting that they probably will not lead to prosecutions in Russia.

2005-10-28.title: Iraq's main Shiite parties agree to run together as electoral bloc
UN warns quake relief efforts may be scaled down
EU offers deeper cuts in tariffs to save talks

2005-10-29.title: U.N. Official Says Airdrops to Quake Area May Be Halted
2005-10-29.abstract: Airdrops of food, tents and other urgent aid to earthquake survivors will be suspended in a matter of days if there is not an immediate infusion of donations, the United Nations warned on Friday.      Nearly three weeks after the Oct. 8 earthquake flattened entire cities and hamlets across northern Pakistan, killing more than 53,000 people and leaving up to three million homeless, humanitarian relief has been hamstrung by a severe shortage of funds. Of the $550 million that the United Nations says it needs, only 20 percent has been pledged, and an even smaller fraction has been delivered.

2005-10-29.title: Briefly: Parties rush to register for December elections
World Briefing | Asia: Japan: Party Approves A Bigger Role For The Military
2005-10-29.abstract: The governing Liberal Democratic Party approved the draft of a plan to amend the country's pacifist Constitution to declare the Self-Defense Forces a real military and to make it easier to dispatch troops overseas. The party, which has sought to change the United States-imposed Constitution since its founding half a century ago, is expected to formally announce the draft at its 50th anniversary next month. The revision of the Constitution would have to pass two-thirds of Parliament and a national referendum.   Norimitsu Onishi (NYT)

2005-10-29.title: World Briefing | Asia: China's Leader In A Rare Visit To North Korea
2005-10-29.abstract: President Hu Jintao of China traveled to North Korea to meet with Kim Jong Il, the country's reclusive leader, raising expectations that the two sides would discuss the standoff over North Korea's nuclear weapons program. Chinese state news media quoted officials as saying that the visit by the Chinese leader, the first since 2001, was aimed at discussing a wide range of economic and political issues, including the upcoming continuation of six-nation nuclear negotiations. China supplies crucial food and energy supplies to North Korea and has been pressing it to make a deal to scrap its atomic weapons.   Joseph Kahn (NYT)

2005-10-30.title: U.S. Quietly Issues Estimate of Iraqi Civilian Casualties
2005-10-30.abstract: In the first public disclosure that the United States military is tracking some of the deaths of Iraqi civilians, the military has released rough figures for Iraqis who have been killed or wounded by insurgents since Jan. 1 last year.      The estimate of dead and wounded Iraqi civilians and security forces was provided by the Pentagon in a report to Congress this month.

2005-10-30.title: Insurgents launch new attacks in Iraq
Group Claims Responsibility for New Delhi Blasts
2005-10-30.abstract: A little-known militant outfit in the disputed Kashmir province took responsibility for the string of bombs that killed 59 people.

2005-10-30.title: Two U.S. Soldiers Charged With Assaulting Detainees
2005-10-30.abstract: Two American soldiers have been charged with assaulting two Afghan detainees and could face court-martial in Afghanistan.

2005-10-30.title: Insurgents kill brother of top Shiite politician

2005-10-31.title: Bush picks Alito for Supreme Court
India Asks Pakistan to Act Against Terror Groups
2005-10-31.abstract: NEW DELHI, Oct. 31 - Two days after a deadly series of bombs tore through the Indian capital, Prime Minister Manmohan Singh told Pakistan's president, Pervez Musharraf, today that investigators had found signs that terror groups outside the country might have been involved, and that Pakistan must act to stifle terrorism against India.

2005-10-31.title: Allawi Tries to Regain Office With a Non-Theocratic Bloc
2005-10-31.abstract: Campaigning for Dec 15 parliamentary elections in Iraq kicks off with eclectic gathering of allies; former prime minister Ayad Allawi tells Communist leaders, Arab nationalists, tribal sheiks and imams that he would lead their coalition to victory; disenchantment with religious Shiite rule is growing, both on streets of Iraq and in Bush administration; this offers secular leaders like Allawi, a White House favorite, reasonable chance of taking some of religious Shiites' majority share of seats (M)

2005-10-31.title: Residents of New Delhi defiant after bombings
Chinese Upbeat After Talks With North Korea
2005-10-31.abstract: Chinese officials say North Korean leader Kim Jong Il has told Pres Hu Jintao of China that he is committed to ending North's nuclear weapons program and that he will push forward with multinational negotiations on the matter soon; upbeat assessment of talks is issued shortly after Hu concludes state visit to North Korea; photo (M)
